name: creating-claude-rules
description: Use when creating or fixing .claude/rules/ files - provides correct paths frontmatter (not globs), glob patterns, and avoids Cursor-specific fields like alwaysApply
capabilities: Create path-scoped rules using correct YAML frontmatter with paths field
Apply global rules by omitting frontmatter entirely
Differentiate between Claude paths format and Cursor globs format
Use glob patterns to match specific file types and directories
Organize rules in subdirectories for better project structure
Avoid Cursor-specific fields like alwaysApply in Claude rules
author: AgentWorkforce
securitySummary: Pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidance for structuring .claude/rules/ files. Contains no executable code, no network calls, no file system access, and no external command execution. All 44 static detections are false positives triggered by documentation text, code examples, and documentation URLs.
valueStatement: Define modular rules that guide Claude on file-specific conventions. Rules in .claude/rules/ automatically apply to matching files, ensuring consistent code patterns across your project.
userTitle: Create Claude Rules for project-specific guidance
limitations: Does not auto-generate rule files - provides guidance only
Does not validate existing rule files for correctness
Does not migrate rules between different projects
No integration with external rule repositories or shared rule libraries

useCases.title: Enforce project conventions
useCases.description: Create rules that ensure consistent code patterns across all team members for TypeScript, React, and testing files.

useCases.title: Document coding standards
useCases.description: Define clear guidelines for specific file types that load automatically when working with matching files.

useCases.title: Convert Cursor rules

promptTemplates.title: Create path-scoped rule
promptTemplates.scenario: Need a rule for specific files
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Claude rule for API TypeScript files that enforces proper error handling patterns.

promptTemplates.title: Define global rule
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a global Claude rule that requires all JavaScript files to use ES module syntax.

promptTemplates.title: Fix frontmatter errors
promptTemplates.prompt: Fix this Claude rule that uses globs instead of paths frontmatter.

promptTemplates.title: Organize rule structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me organize my .claude/rules/ directory with subdirectories for frontend and backend rules.

bestPractices: Use quoted paths patterns for reliability across different platforms
Group related rules in subdirectories for better organization
Keep rules focused on specific file types or patterns
Omit frontmatter entirely for rules that apply globally to all files
antiPatterns: Using globs field instead of paths - this is Cursor format, not Claude
Including alwaysApply field - Claude uses omit-paths approach instead
Leaving paths patterns unquoted - may fail on some platforms
Creating too many narrow rules - consolidate where possible

faq.question: What is the difference between paths and globs?
faq.answer: Claude uses paths field while Cursor uses globs. Use paths for Claude rules in .claude/rules/.

faq.question: How do I create a global rule?
faq.answer: Omit the frontmatter entirely. The rule applies to all files without any path restrictions.

faq.question: Can I use subdirectories in .claude/rules?
faq.answer: Yes. Rules are discovered recursively. Use subdirectories to organize by feature or file type.

faq.question: Is my data safe with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill contains only documentation. It does not read, write, or transmit any files or data.

faq.question: Why is my rule not applying to my files?
faq.answer: Check that frontmatter uses paths (not globs), patterns are quoted, and patterns match your file paths correctly.

faq.question: How does this compare to CLAUDE.md?
faq.answer: CLAUDE.md applies project-wide. Rules in .claude/rules/ are modular and apply only to files matching their path patterns.
